user_goals_summary: This user writes internal technical documentation for experienced engineers. They prioritize explaining design rationale and rejected alternatives over describing system behavior, expect code examples to confirm what prose already taught rather than introduce new concepts, and care deeply about the reader's navigation experience and emotional respect when encountering difficult material.

rubric.name: Design Rationale with Named Alternatives
rubric.description: Every design decision should be a design argument: not just what the system does and why, but what alternatives were considered and what specific problems they would have created. This is what teaches the reader the system's design philosophy and helps them make good decisions when their use case doesn't fit the happy path.

dimensions.label: Each significant design decision names at least one alternative that was considered or rejected
Explains the specific problems or tradeoffs the rejected alternatives would have introduced
Explains why the system works this way, not just what it does — the 'what' the reader can get from the API
The rationale equips the reader to reason about edge cases and make decisions when things go wrong

rubric.name: Code-Prose Complementarity
rubric.description: Prose does all the explanatory work; code is the moment everything clicks, not a second tutorial. Code examples should show the core sequence in the tightest possible form, trusting that the prose already covered what each step means. If the code needs scaffolding or verbose comments to make sense on its own, the prose above it didn't do its job.

dimensions.label: Prose fully explains concepts and flow before code appears
Code demonstrates what prose already explained rather than introducing new concepts
Code uses the minimum scaffolding needed to show the core sequence — no wrapper functions or boilerplate that exist only for the example
Inline code comments reinforce or anchor the reader in the flow rather than re-explaining from scratch

rubric.name: Warning and Gotcha Placement
rubric.description: Warnings earn their place by landing where the mistake is about to happen — right before the reader writes or executes the relevant logic. A warning buried in an explanation paragraph forces the reader to scroll back when they hit the error, which means the docs didn't do their job. Each warning should also name the specific failure mode and what makes it hard to debug.

dimensions.label: Warnings appear immediately before the point where the reader will write or execute the relevant logic
Each warning names the specific failure scenario rather than offering a generic 'be careful'
Explains what makes the failure mode difficult to identify or recover from

rubric.name: Narrative-Driven Section Structure
rubric.description: The visual structure of a section should reflect the logical structure of its content and give the reader a sense of momentum. Headers should feel like questions the reader is already holding — each one answering something the previous section raised — not just topic labels. Someone returning to the doc should be able to locate a specific topic by scanning headers alone.

dimensions.label: Section headers give a sense of progression, with each one feeling like it follows from the previous
Headers are framed as questions or concerns the reader is already holding, resembling their internal monologue
Someone returning to the doc can locate a specific topic by scanning headers alone
The visual divisions in the section match the logical divisions in the content

rubric.name: Reader-Respectful Tone Around Difficulty
rubric.description: When the documentation describes something genuinely tricky, the tone should treat the difficulty as inherent to the problem, not as a gap in the reader's experience. Language should never imply that a failure mode or concept 'should have been obvious.' State difficulty plainly — name what makes it hard to reason about — and trust that the reader is competent.

dimensions.label: Describes genuinely tricky concepts as inherently difficult rather than things the reader should have caught
Avoids language that implies failure modes should have been obvious or that softens genuine complexity
States plainly what makes something hard to reason about rather than hedging or hand-waving
Assumes the reader is technically capable and addresses them as a peer

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every design decision must name rejected alternatives and their specific downsides — stating what the system does without explaining what it was chosen over is treated as fundamentally incomplete. This user iterates on exactly one concern per round and never reverts prior approvals; when they say something is done, focus only on the newly flagged issue. Tone precision is extremely high-stakes: the difference between 'non-obvious failure mode' and 'genuinely hard to reason about' warranted a full revision round — language around difficulty must validate the reader's experience, never imply they should have known better.

This is what teaches the reader the system's design philosophy and helps them make good decisions when their use case doesn't fit the happy path.

dimensions.label: Each significant design decision names at least one alternative that was considered or rejected
Explains the specific problems or tradeoffs the rejected alternatives would have introduced
Explains why the system works this way, not just what it does — the 'what' the reader can get from the API
The rationale equips the reader to reason about edge cases and make decisions when things go wrong

dimensions.label: Warnings appear immediately before the point where the reader will write or execute the relevant logic

rubric.name: Prose Economy and Sentence-Level Precision
rubric.description: Every sentence should do distinct work. Padding, clever-sounding summaries that don't add information, and sentences that restate what was just said in different words all signal that the draft has not been tightened enough. A quip or memorable turn of phrase is not a substitute for a precisely placed warning or a concrete decision rule. The test: if a sentence were deleted, would the reader lose something they needed?

dimensions.label: No sentence restates what an adjacent sentence already said
Clever or memorable phrasings are not used as substitutes for concrete guidance or warnings
Decision rules and heuristics are stated concretely enough to apply without interpretation
Each sentence would leave a gap if removed — none exist purely for rhetorical effect

user_goals_summary: This user writes internal technical documentation for experienced engineers. They prioritize explaining design rationale and rejected alternatives over describing system behavior, expect code examples to confirm what prose already taught rather than introduce new concepts, and care deeply about the reader's navigation experience and emotional respect when encountering difficult material.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: every design decision must name rejected alternatives and their specific downsides — stating what the system does without explaining what it was chosen over is treated as fundamentally incomplete. This user iterates on exactly one concern per round and never reverts prior approvals; when they say something is done, focus only on the newly flagged issue. Tone precision is extremely high-stakes: the difference between 'non-obvious failure mode' and 'genuinely hard to reason about' warranted a full revision round — language around difficulty must validate the reader's experience, never imply they should have known better.

user_goals_summary: Docs should teach the reader the system's design philosophy — not just what it does, but why it works this way and what was rejected. When something is genuinely hard, the writing should name the source of difficulty plainly and respect the reader's competence, never implying they should have seen it coming. Structure, code, and warnings should all be precisely placed to serve the reader at the moment they need it.

rubric.name: Design Rationale with Named Alternatives
rubric.description: Every design decision should be a design argument: not just what the system does and why, but what alternatives were rejected and what specific problems they would have created. Name the mistaken pattern the reader is likely to reach for and explain why it fails. Use genuinely ambiguous boundary cases to teach judgment, not easy examples with obvious answers.

dimensions.label: Each significant design decision names at least one alternative that was considered or rejected
Explains the specific problems or tradeoffs the rejected alternatives would have introduced
Explains why the system works this way, not just what it does — the 'what' the reader can get from the API
Names the specific wrong approach the reader is likely to reach for and explains why it fails in practice
Uses genuinely ambiguous examples that sit at the real decision boundary rather than cases with obvious answers

rubric.name: Honest Treatment of Difficulty
rubric.description: When something is genuinely hard, locate the difficulty in the problem itself — not in missing information, not in the reader's experience, not in a gap that better tooling would close. Some decisions are genuine judgment calls where both answers are defensible and the information won't make the choice for you. Say so plainly. Never imply a failure mode or complexity 'should have been obvious' or is just 'harder than it sounds.'

dimensions.label: Locates difficulty in the inherent nature of the problem, not in gaps in the reader's information or experience
Avoids phrases like 'harder than it sounds,' 'non-obvious,' or 'tricky' that imply the reader should eventually find it easy
States plainly what makes something hard to reason about — names the specific mechanism rather than just flagging that it's complex
When a decision is a genuine judgment call with defensible answers on both sides, says so rather than implying more information would resolve it
Treats the reader as someone standing in front of a real system with real constraints, not someone who needs reassurance

rubric.name: Warning and Gotcha Placement
rubric.description: Warnings earn their place by landing where the mistake is about to happen — right before the reader writes or executes the relevant logic, or before they've gotten comfortable with the wrong choice. Each warning should name the specific failure mode and what makes it hard to debug or recover from.

dimensions.label: Warnings appear immediately before the point where the reader will write or execute the relevant logic
Each warning names the specific failure scenario rather than offering a generic 'be careful'
Explains what makes the failure mode difficult to identify or recover from
Warnings land before the reader has committed to a path, not as retrospective observations or after-the-fact summaries

rubric.description: The visual structure should reflect the logical structure and give the reader a sense of momentum. Headers should feel like questions the reader is already holding — resembling their internal monologue — with each one answering something the previous section raised. The reader should feel walked through a thought process, not handed separate verdicts to check off.

dimensions.label: Section headers give a sense of progression, with each one feeling like it follows from the previous
Headers are framed as questions or concerns the reader is already holding, resembling their internal monologue
Someone returning to the doc can locate a specific topic by scanning headers alone
The overall structure reads as one argument building toward understanding, not separate blocks of reasoning stacked together

Decision rules and heuristics are stated concretely enough to apply without interpretation

coaching_notes: This user iterates relentlessly on tone — particularly on how difficulty and complexity are described. They will reject formulations that locate difficulty in missing information, timing gaps, or the reader's experience; they want difficulty located in the problem itself. The word 'defensible' is preferred over 'valid' or 'correct' when describing judgment calls. They treat code as the payoff moment, not the explanation — if code is doing explanatory work, they read it as a failure of the prose. They strongly prefer naming rejected alternatives and anti-patterns over simply stating the right answer, because 'the rejected path is where the actual learning is.'

rubric.name: Honest Treatment of Difficulty

dimensions.label: Locates difficulty in the inherent nature of the problem, not in gaps in the reader's information or experience

rubric.name: Validating Reader Experience
rubric.description: When correcting a likely wrong instinct, treat the reader's prior experience as the source of that instinct, not the source of an error. Validate that the approach they're reaching for is exactly right in other contexts before explaining why this system is different. Write directly to the reader's experience — speak to them in the moment they're in, not about them from a distance.

dimensions.label: Frames the reader's wrong instinct as coming from competence in other systems, not from ignorance or carelessness
Acknowledges the reader's likely approach as reasonable before explaining why it doesn't apply here
Addresses the reader directly in their experience ('you'll know it's clicked when...') rather than describing them from the outside ('the adjustment will happen')
Lists the specific things the reader will be tempted to do (e.g., checking expiry themselves, wrapping the SDK) rather than vaguely warning against deviation

rubric.name: Warning and Gotcha Placement
rubric.description: Warnings earn their place by landing where the mistake is about to happen — right before the reader writes or executes the relevant logic, or before they've mentally committed to a wrong pattern. Each warning should name the specific failure mode and what makes it hard to debug or recover from.

dimensions.label: Warnings appear immediately before the point where the reader will write or execute the relevant logic
Each warning names the specific failure scenario rather than offering a generic 'be careful'

rubric.name: Narrative-Driven Section Structure
rubric.description: The visual structure should reflect the logical structure and give the reader a sense of momentum. Headers should tell a progression story — each one answering something the previous section raised — so the reader feels walked through a thought process rather than handed separate verdicts. The document should close on something that belongs to the reader's experience in this document, not hand off to another one.

dimensions.label: Section headers give a sense of progression, with each one feeling like it follows from the previous
Header style matches the doc's context and conventions — whether framed as reader questions, reasoning steps, or diagnostic labels
Someone returning to the doc can locate a specific topic by scanning headers alone
The overall structure reads as one argument building toward understanding, not separate blocks of reasoning stacked together
The document ends on something that belongs to the reader's experience here — not a redirect, logistics pointer, or handoff to another doc

rubric.name: Code-Prose Complementarity
rubric.description: Prose does all the explanatory work; code is the moment everything clicks, not a second tutorial. Code examples should show the core sequence in the tightest possible form — a gut-check moment where the reader recognizes their own situation. Each snippet should be self-contained when skimmed, anchored by a single comment that orients without re-explaining.

dimensions.label: Prose fully explains concepts and flow before code appears
Code demonstrates what prose already explained rather than introducing new concepts
Code uses the minimum scaffolding needed to show the core sequence — no wrapper functions or boilerplate that exist only for the example
Each code snippet includes a brief anchoring comment that makes it self-contained for someone skimming, without duplicating the prose

coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: never imply difficulty comes from the reader's inexperience — locate it in the problem itself. This user's preferred trade-off is spending more words on 'why this is hard' and 'what we rejected' over exhaustive 'how-to' detail. They iterate most intensely on tone at moments of correction — where the doc tells the reader their instinct is wrong — and on endings, which must land in the document rather than hand off. When offering options, provide 2-3 with a clear recommendation; this user has strong register preferences and decides quickly when given concrete alternatives.

rubric.name: Honest Treatment of Difficulty
rubric.description: When something is genuinely hard, locate the difficulty in the problem itself — not in missing information, not in the reader's experience level, not in a gap that better visibility would close. Some decisions are genuine judgment calls where both answers are defensible and the information won't make the choice for you. Say so plainly. The doc should sit with the tension rather than rush past it.

dimensions.label: Locates difficulty in the inherent nature of the problem, not in gaps in the reader's information or experience
Avoids phrases like 'harder than it sounds,' 'non-obvious,' or 'tricky' that imply the reader should eventually find it easy
States plainly what makes something hard to reason about — names the specific mechanism rather than just flagging that it's complex
When a decision is a genuine judgment call with defensible answers on both sides, says so rather than implying more information would resolve it
Stays with the difficulty long enough for it to land rather than rushing to resolve it with a correction or answer

rubric.name: Validating Reader Experience
rubric.description: When correcting a likely wrong instinct, treat the reader's prior experience as the source of that instinct, not the source of an error. Validate that the approach they're reaching for is exactly right in other contexts before explaining why this system is different. Write directly to the reader's experience — speak to them in the moment they're in, not about them from a distance.

dimensions.label: Frames the reader's wrong instinct as coming from competence in other systems, not from ignorance or carelessness
Acknowledges the reader's likely approach as reasonable before explaining why it doesn't apply here
Addresses the reader directly in their experience ('you'll know it's clicked when...') rather than describing them from the outside ('the adjustment will happen')
Lists the specific things the reader will be tempted to do (e.g., checking expiry themselves, wrapping the SDK) rather than vaguely warning against deviation

user_goals_summary: Writes internal technical docs for engineers who are competent but unfamiliar with a specific system. Values explaining why a system works this way over what it does, including naming rejected alternatives. Treats difficulty and complexity as inherent to the problem, never as a gap in the reader's competence, and validates the reader's prior experience before correcting course.
coaching_notes: Non-negotiable: never imply difficulty comes from the reader's inexperience — locate it in the problem itself. This user's preferred trade-off is spending more words on 'why this is hard' and 'what we rejected' over exhaustive 'how-to' detail. They iterate most intensely on tone at moments of correction — where the doc tells the reader their instinct is wrong — and on endings, which must land in the document rather than hand off. When offering options, provide 2-3 with a clear recommendation; this user has strong register preferences and decides quickly when given concrete alternatives.

user_goals_summary: Writes internal docs that teach engineers how to make good decisions with a system, not just use it. Every design choice should name what was rejected and why; warnings should land at the exact moment the reader is about to write the wrong code; and when something is genuinely hard, the doc should say so plainly rather than softening, hand-waving, or implying the reader should have seen it coming.

rubric.name: Design Rationale with Named Alternatives
rubric.description: Every design decision should be a design argument: not just what the system does and why, but what alternatives were rejected and what specific problems they would have created. Name the mistaken pattern the reader is likely to reach for and explain why it fails. Use genuinely ambiguous boundary cases to teach judgment, not easy examples with obvious answers.

dimensions.label: Each significant design decision names at least one alternative that was considered or rejected
Explains the specific problems or tradeoffs the rejected alternatives would have introduced
Explains why the system works this way, not just what it does — the 'what' the reader can get from the API
Names the specific wrong approach the reader is likely to reach for and explains why it fails in practice
Uses genuinely ambiguous examples that sit at the real decision boundary rather than cases with obvious answers

rubric.name: Warning and Gotcha Placement
rubric.description: Warnings earn their place by landing where the mistake is about to happen — right before the reader writes or executes the relevant logic, or before they've mentally committed to a wrong pattern. Each warning should name the specific failure mode and what makes it hard to debug or recover from.

dimensions.label: Warnings appear immediately before the point where the reader will write or execute the relevant logic
Each warning names the specific failure scenario rather than offering a generic 'be careful'
Explains what makes the failure mode difficult to identify or recover from
Warnings land before the reader has committed to a path, not as retrospective observations or after-the-fact summaries

rubric.name: Honest Treatment of Difficulty
rubric.description: When something is genuinely hard, locate the difficulty in the problem itself — not in missing information, not in the reader's experience level, not in a gap that better visibility would close. Some decisions are genuine judgment calls where both answers are defensible and the information won't make the choice for you. Say so plainly. The doc should sit with the tension rather than rush past it.

dimensions.label: Locates difficulty in the inherent nature of the problem, not in gaps in the reader's information or experience
Avoids phrases like 'harder than it sounds,' 'non-obvious,' or 'tricky' that imply the reader should eventually find it easy
States plainly what makes something hard to reason about — names the specific mechanism rather than just flagging that it's complex
When a decision is a genuine judgment call with defensible answers on both sides, says so rather than implying more information would resolve it
Stays with the difficulty long enough for it to land rather than rushing to resolve it with a correction or answer

rubric.name: Narrative-Driven Section Structure
rubric.description: The visual structure should reflect the logical structure and give the reader a sense of momentum. Headers should tell a progression story — each one answering something the previous section raised — so the reader feels walked through a thought process rather than handed separate verdicts. Headers at action points should match the stakes and energy of the moment, not go flat when the reader is ready to act. The document should close on something that belongs to the reader's experience in this document, not hand off to another one.

dimensions.label: Section headers give a sense of progression, with each one feeling like it follows from the previous
Header tone and energy match the moment — action-point headers carry weight rather than defaulting to flat labels
Someone returning to the doc can locate a specific topic by scanning headers alone
The overall structure reads as one argument building toward understanding, not separate blocks of reasoning stacked together
The document ends on something that belongs to the reader's experience here — not a redirect, logistics pointer, or handoff to another doc

rubric.name: Validating Reader Experience
rubric.description: When correcting a likely wrong instinct, treat the reader's prior experience as the source of that instinct, not the source of an error. Validate that the approach they're reaching for is exactly right in other contexts before explaining why this system is different. Write directly to the reader's experience — speak to them in the moment they're in, not about them from a distance.

dimensions.label: Frames the reader's wrong instinct as coming from competence in other systems, not from ignorance or carelessness
Acknowledges the reader's likely approach as reasonable before explaining why it doesn't apply here
Addresses the reader directly in their experience ('you'll know it's clicked when...') rather than describing them from the outside ('the adjustment will happen')
Lists the specific things the reader will be tempted to do rather than vaguely warning against deviation

rubric.name: Code-Prose Complementarity
rubric.description: Prose does all the explanatory work; code is the moment everything clicks, not a second tutorial. Code examples should show the core sequence in the tightest possible form — a gut-check moment where the reader recognizes their own situation. Each snippet should be self-contained when skimmed, anchored by a single comment that orients without re-explaining.

dimensions.label: Prose fully explains concepts and flow before code appears
Code demonstrates what prose already explained rather than introducing new concepts
Code uses the minimum scaffolding needed to show the core sequence — no wrapper functions or boilerplate that exist only for the example
Each code snippet includes a brief anchoring comment that makes it self-contained for someone skimming, without duplicating the prose

coaching_notes: This user iterates most persistently on tone and framing — they'll accept a first draft's content but push for 5-10 rounds on how difficulty is described and how the reader is addressed. Non-negotiables: every design choice must name the rejected alternative; warnings must precede action points; difficulty must be located in the problem itself, never in the reader. Preferred trade-off: will always sacrifice brevity to sit honestly with complexity rather than gloss over it. When stuck on a subjective choice (headers, phrasing), present 2-3 options with distinct tradeoffs and let them pick — they give very precise directional feedback and know what they want when they see it.

rubric.name: Design Rationale with Named Alternatives
rubric.description: Every design decision should be a design argument: not just what the system does and why, but what alternatives were rejected and what specific problems they would have created. Name the mistaken pattern the reader is likely to reach for and explain why it fails. Use genuinely ambiguous boundary cases to teach judgment, not easy examples with obvious answers.

dimensions.label: Each significant design decision names at least one alternative that was considered or rejected
Explains the specific problems or tradeoffs the rejected alternatives would have introduced
Explains why the system works this way, not just what it does — the 'what' the reader can get from the API
Names the specific wrong approach the reader is likely to reach for and explains why it fails in practice
Uses genuinely ambiguous examples that sit at the real decision boundary rather than cases with obvious answers
